instruction: Polish the following economics paper abstract into top-journal style. Improve academic rigor, sentence structure, and precision. Keep the core content intact. Target length: 150-180 words.

input.draft_abstract: This paper studies how minimum wage increase affects employment. We use data from China to analyze this question. We find that when minimum wage goes up, employment goes down a little bit. The effect is bigger for young workers and low-skilled workers. We use some econometric methods to make our result reliable. Our findings suggest that policymakers should be careful when raising minimum wages.
input.context: Study uses city-level panel data 2005-2018, DiD design exploiting staggered minimum wage increases across provinces.

reference_output: This paper estimates the employment effects of minimum wage increases in China using city-level panel data spanning 2005 to 2018. Exploiting the staggered timing of provincial minimum wage adjustments in a difference-in-differences framework, we find that a 10% increase in the minimum wage reduces total employment by approximately 1.2%, with effects concentrated among young workers (ages 16–24) and low-skilled workers without tertiary education. The estimated employment elasticity of −0.12 falls within the range documented in the literature for developing economies, though it is larger in magnitude than comparable estimates for the United States. Robustness checks confirm that the results are not driven by pre-existing differential trends, selective compliance, or concurrent policy changes. These findings suggest that minimum wage policy in China involves a meaningful employment–wage tradeoff, particularly for vulnerable labor market groups, and highlight the importance of targeting complementary labor market policies to offset distributional costs.

instruction: Polish the following economics paper abstract into top-journal style. Improve academic rigor, English expression, and structure. Keep the core content intact. Target length: 150-180 words.

input.draft_abstract: We look at whether education level has effect on people's health in rural China. We collected survey data and did regression. We found that people who have more education are healthier. But we are not sure if this is causal. We used instrumental variable which is years of education policy change to deal with this problem. The IV result shows education still has positive effect on health. So education is important for health policy.
input.context: Study uses CFPS rural sample 2010-2018, instruments education with cohort exposure to 1986 Compulsory Education Law.

reference_output: This paper examines the causal effect of education on adult health outcomes among rural residents in China. Using six waves of the China Family Panel Studies (CFPS) from 2010 to 2018, we instrument individual years of schooling with cohort-level exposure to the 1986 Compulsory Education Law, which induced sharp increases in educational attainment among affected birth cohorts. OLS estimates indicate a positive association between education and self-reported health, physical functioning, and chronic disease prevalence, but may be upward-biased by unobserved health endowments. Two-stage least squares estimates confirm a positive and statistically significant causal effect: one additional year of schooling reduces the probability of reporting poor health by 3.8 percentage points (p < 0.01). Effects are larger for women and households in below-median income villages. These findings underscore the health externalities of educational investment and suggest that policies expanding schooling access in rural China can yield compounding welfare gains beyond labor market returns.

instruction: Polish the following economics paper abstract into top-journal style. Improve academic rigor, English expression, and structure. Keep the core content intact. Target length: 150-180 words.

input.draft_abstract: Trade liberalization is very important for China's economy development. After China joined WTO in 2001, many firms can now export to other countries. This paper studies if trade affects innovation of Chinese firms. We use firm data and tariff data together. We find that when tariff fall, firms do more innovation activity like patenting. This effect is stronger for firms in competitive industries. We think this is because competition force firms to innovate.
input.context: Study uses Chinese Industrial Survey data 2000-2007 merged with HS-level tariff schedules, exploits WTO accession as source of tariff variation.

reference_output: This paper examines the effect of trade liberalization on firm-level innovation in China, exploiting China's accession to the World Trade Organization in 2001 as a source of plausibly exogenous variation in import tariff schedules. Merging the Chinese Annual Industrial Survey (2000–2007) with product-level tariff data, we construct firm-level exposure to tariff reductions and estimate their effect on patenting activity and R&D expenditure. We find that a 10 percentage point reduction in input tariffs increases patent filings by 6.3% and R&D intensity by 0.4 percentage points. The innovation response is significantly stronger among firms operating in industries with initially higher domestic competition, consistent with the escape-competition mechanism proposed by Aghion et al. (2005). Effects are driven primarily by firms in coastal provinces with pre-existing export capacity, suggesting that global market access amplifies innovation incentives. These findings contribute to the debate on trade policy and technological upgrading, with implications for industrial policy design in large developing economies.

instruction: Polish the following economics paper abstract into top-journal style. Improve academic rigor, English expression, and structure. Keep the core content intact. Target length: 150-180 words.

input.draft_abstract: This paper is about fiscal consolidation and firm investment. We use data from many places and years and run regressions. The main result is that the effect is important and statistically significant. We also find some groups have bigger effects than others. We try to solve endogeneity with macro panel DID, and we do robustness checks. The paper is useful because policy makers care about this issue.
constraints: Mention the topic: fiscal consolidation and firm investment
Preserve the method: macro panel DID
Report a clear causal estimate in polished wording
Include heterogeneity and policy implications without overclaiming
input.context: Study uses macro panel DID with administrative or survey panel data and multiple robustness checks.
